records.ety: [F. crdit (cf. It. credito), L. creditum loan, prop. neut. of creditus, p. p. of credere to trust, loan, believe. See Creed.]

defs: Reliance on the truth of something said or done; belief; faith; trust; confidence.
quotes: When Jonathan and the people heard these words they gave no credit unto them, nor received them.

defs: Reputation derived from the confidence of others; esteem; honor; good name; estimation.
quotes: John Gilpin was a citizen Of credit and renown.

defs: A ground of, or title to, belief or confidence; authority derived from character or reputation.
quotes: The things which we properly believe, be only such as are received on the credit of divine testimony.

defs: That which tends to procure, or add to, reputation or esteem; an honor.
quotes: I published, because I was told I might please such as it was a credit to please.

defs: Influence derived from the good opinion, confidence, or favor of others; interest.
quotes: Having credit enough with his master to provide for his own interest.

defs: Trust given or received; expectation of future payment for property transferred, or of fulfillment or promises given; mercantile reputation entitling one to be trusted; -- applied to individuals, corporations, communities, or nations; as, to buy goods on credit.
quotes: Credit is nothing but the expectation of money, within some limited time.

defs: The time given for payment for lands or goods sold on trust; as, a long credit or a short credit.

The side of an account on which are entered all items reckoned as values received from the party or the category named at the head of the account; also, any one, or the sum, of these items; -- the opposite of debit; as, this sum is carried to one's credit, and that to his debit; A has several credits on the books of B.
quotes: He touched the dead corpse of Public Credit, and it sprung upon its feet.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Credited; p. pr. & vb. n. Crediting.]

defs: To confide in the truth of; to give credence to; to put trust in; to believe.
quotes: How shall they credit A poor unlearned virgin?

defs: To bring honor or repute upon; to do credit to; to raise the estimation of.
quotes: You credit the church as much by your government as you did the school formerly by your wit.

defs: To enter upon the credit side of an account; to give credit for; as, to credit the amount paid; to set to the credit of; as, to credit a man with the interest paid on a bond.
quotes: Crove, Helmholtz, and Meyer, are more than any others to be credited with the clear enunciation of this doctrine.
